---
title: "Webscraping Tutorial with books.toscrape.com"
format: docx
author: Rachel Saidi
---

# Webscraping with books.toscrape.com

[![Website to teach webscraping](images/bookstoscrape-01.jpg){width="391"}](books.toscrape.com)

## Install necessary packages for this project

```{r message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

#install.packages('rvest')
library(rvest)
library(tidyverse)
#install.packages("robotstxt")
library(robotstxt)
```

### Use the following URL for our webscraping tutorial

https://books.toscrape.com/


## Inspect the html and css code on the website

- use right click "inspect" to get to the website code

## This tutorial walks through the steps from setup to export:

1. **Load the data and fetch the webpage** - `read_html()` downloads and parses the HTML
2. **Inspect selectors** — understand which CSS selectors map to each data field
3. **Extract fields** — titles, ratings, prices, stock status, and image URLs
5. **Build a data frame** — combine everything with `tibble()`
6. **Paginate** — loop through all 50 pages to collect all 1,000 books
7. **Export** — save to CSV with `write.csv()`

A few things worth highlighting for students:

- The **rating** is a CSS class word ("One", "Two", etc.), not a number — so we need `gsub()` to clean it, and the bonus section shows how to convert it to numeric.

- The **image URL** is a relative path on the site, so we replace the `../../` prefix with the full base URL.

- `Sys.sleep(0.5)` in the pagination loop is a good habit — it's called *polite scraping* and avoids overloading the server.

## Fetch the HTML

Use read_html() to download and parse the page. Think of it as loading the page source into R so you can query it.


## Step 1 - Load the data and fetch the webpage

```{r}
# Store the URL
url <- "https://books.toscrape.com"
paths_allowed(url) # checks whether the website allows webscraping
```



```{r}
# Read and parse the HTML
webpage <- read_html(url)
```

## Step 2 - Inspect the selectors 

View the structure of your webpage - check what it looks like in your global environment


## Inspect & select

Understand the CSS selectors
Before extracting data, you need the right CSS selectors. Here's what to look for on books.toscrape.com:

article.product_pod — each book card
h3 > a — book title
p.star-rating — star rating
p.price_color — price
p.availability — stock status
img.thumbnail — book image


## Step 3 - Extract all the fields (columns of information)

Each book lives inside an article.product_pod element


```{r}
books <- webpage |>
  html_elements("article.product_pod")
```


# How many books are on this page?

```{r}
length(books)
```

*Should be 20*


Scrape titles, ratings, prices, stock, and images
Now extract each data field from within the book nodes. We use html_element() (singular) to get the first match inside each book card.

## Book titles


```{r}
# Titles are in the "title" attribute of the <a> tag inside <h3>
titles <- books |>
  html_element("h3 > a") |>
  html_attr("title")
head(titles)
```


## Star ratings

Ratings are stored as a CSS class: "One", "Two", "Three" etc. We need to extract the class string and clean it

```{r}
ratings_raw <- books |>
  html_element("p.star-rating") |>
  html_attr("class")
head(ratings_raw)
```



## Remove "star-rating " prefix to isolate the word

Use gsub

```{r}
ratings <- gsub("star-rating", "", ratings_raw)|>
  trimws()
head(ratings)
```

## Prices

```{r}
# Prices sit in a <p> with class "price_color"
prices <- books |>
  html_element("p.price_color") |>
  html_text2()
head(prices)
```

## Clean the prices 

Remove the "£" symbol and ensure the value is numeric

```{r}
prices <- as.numeric(gsub("£", "", prices))
head(prices)
```


## Stock status

Availability is in a <p> with class "availability" - Use trimws() to remove surrounding whitespace

```{r}
stock <- books |>
  html_element("p.availability") |>
  html_text2() |>
  trimws()
head(stock)
```

## Book images

Images use the "src" attribute on <img class="thumbnail">. We need to prefix the base URL since src is a relative path


```{r}
images <- books |>
  html_element("img.thumbnail") |>
  html_attr("src") |>
  gsub("../../", "https://books.toscrape.com/", x = _)
head(images)
```



Why do we need to fix the image URL? 

The src attribute on this site uses a relative path like ../../media/cache/.... We replace the ../../ with the base URL so the links actually work.


## Step 5 — Build a clean data frame


Combine all five vectors into a single tidy data frame using tibble() (from dplyr) to test if you have scraped everything properly so far.

```{r}
test_df <- tibble(
  title  = titles,
  rating = ratings,
  price  = prices,
  stock  = stock,
  image  = images
)
head(test_df)
```


## Step 6 — Paginate

Scrape all 50 pages

The site has 50 pages of books. Wrap the scraping logic in a loop that builds the URL for each page and binds the results together.

```{r}
# Empty list to collect each page's data
all_books <- list()

for (i in 1:50) {

  # Page 1 uses the root URL; pages 2+ use /catalogue/page-N.html
  if (i == 1) {
    url <- "https://books.toscrape.com"
  } else {
    url <- paste0("https://books.toscrape.com/catalogue/page-", i, ".html")
  }

  page  <- read_html(url)
  books <- html_elements(page, "article.product_pod")

  all_books[[i]] <- tibble(
    title  = html_attr(html_element(books, "h3 > a"), "title"),
    rating = gsub("star-rating ", "", html_attr(html_element(books, "p.star-rating"), "class")),
    price  = html_text2(html_element(books, "p.price_color")),
    stock  = trimws(html_text2(html_element(books, "p.availability"))),
    image  = gsub("../../", "https://books.toscrape.com/",
               html_attr(html_element(books, "img.thumbnail"), "src"))
  )

  # Be polite — pause briefly between requests
  Sys.sleep(0.5)
  cat("Scraped page", i, "\n")
}
```


## Combine all pages into one data frame

```{r}
full_df <- bind_rows(all_books)
nrow(full_df)  # Should be 1000
```


## Why Sys.sleep()? 

Adding a small delay between requests is considered polite scraping. It avoids hammering the server and mimics human browsing behavior — a good habit to build from the start.


## Step 7 — Optional Export

Export your final dataset as a CSV file to use in analysis, reports, or other tools.

```{r}
getwd()
# Save to your working directory
write_csv(full_df, "bookstoscrape.csv")
```


## Now explore the data

Remove the "£" symbol and ensure the value is numeric

```{r}
full_df$price <- as.numeric(gsub("£", "", full_df$price))
head(full_df)
```

## Convert rating words to numbers

```{r}
rating_map <- c(One=1, Two=2, Three=3, Four=4, Five=5)
full_df1 <- full_df |>
  mutate(rating_num = rating_map[rating])
```


## Count books per rating

```{r}
table1 <- full_df1 |> 
  count(rating, sort = TRUE)
table1
```


## Average price by star rating

```{r}
full_df1 |>
  group_by(rating) |>
  summarise(avg_price = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_price))
```

## Problem 1: Based on the scraped books_to_scrape data frame, create a scatterplot that shows rating_num on the x-axis and price on the y-axis.

-   Add a regression line - change the default color of the line, remove the standard error band, and make it dot-dash.
-   Be sure to provide a title, axis labels, and caption for the data source
-   Change the default ggplot background theme

```{r}
# scatterplot
#p1 <-

```

## Problem 2: Create a bar graph

Use any of the information from the full_df1 or any of the aggregated tables. 

- Be sure to include title, axis labels, and caption for the data source
-   Change the default ggplot background theme



```{r}
# bar graph
#p2 <-


```



## Problem 3: Create one more plot of your own

Create a different plot than plot 1 of your choice.

-   Be sure to show at least 3 colors.
-   Be sure to provide a title, axis labels, and caption for the data source
-   Change the default ggplot background theme

```{r}
#p3 <-
```


```{r}
#install.packages("patchwork")
#library(patchwork)
# p1 + p2 +p3
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------
